root_goal: how to visualize the data of air pollution

args.query_intent: how to visualize the data of air pollution

orchestrator_plan.reason: 使用用户原始问题作为初始检索条件，获取相关文献基础
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The semantic‑search run was aimed at gathering concrete visualizations that could answer the user’s request to “visualize the data of air pollution.” The retrieved set is dominated by figure‑centric evidence: concentration‑profile plots that trace CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, O₃ and particulate matter along a tunnel (illustrating accumulation and mixing zones); vertical cross‑sections of particle number and surface‑area concentrations up‑ and down‑wind of a motorway, which highlight height‑dependent dispersion and strong linear ties to NOₓ; regional bar charts comparing AQI, PM₂.₅, SO₂ and NO₂ across eastern and western study areas, thereby exposing spatial heterogeneity; and correlation‑coefficient graphics that link socioeconomic growth (GDP per‑capita) to PM₂.₅ reductions. Complementary spatial visualizations include high‑resolution 0.25° × 0.25° emission grids from the MIX Asian inventory (showing hotspots for SO₂, NOₓ, CO, etc.) and a CONUS domain map marking urban/rural zones and ozonesonde stations for model context. A methodological illustration of spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I) applied in ArcGIS/GeoDa further underscores the analytic tools available for mapping air‑quality patterns. Together, these results furnish a rich repertoire of maps, plots, and charts that directly address the visualization need, while also flagging a few ancillary items (data‑source notices, title‑only entries) that are less immediately useful.

content.text: Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing. The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.

According to the  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \\mathrm{SO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  results, a zonation of the tunnel could be established: mixing zone (entrance to  $600\\mathrm{m}$ ) and accumulation zone ( $600\\mathrm{m}$  to exit). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  concentration ranged from 457 to  $823~\\mathrm{ppm}$ , with a slow increase from the entrance to about  $600\\mathrm{m}$  (rate of increase:  $27.6~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ) and a rapid increase from  $600\\mathrm{m}$  to the exit (rate of increase:  $207~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  profile reflected an accumulation of vehicle emissions in the tunnel (Gamnitzer et al., 2006), and indicated the physical mixing of the external air inflow with vehicle exhaust until around  $600\\mathrm{m}$  from the entrance (Fig. 2a). The  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  concentration ranged from 3.06 to  $53.2~\\mathrm{ppb}$  and the rate of increase in the mixing and accumulation zone was 5.42"], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: how to visualize the data of air pollution

evaluation.extracted_insight: Concentration profiles of CO₂, SO₂, NO, NO₂, O₃, PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅ increase with distance from the tunnel entrance, revealing a mixing zone up to ~600 m and an accumulation zone beyond, with CO₂ rising from 457 ppm to 823 ppm (27.6 ppm km⁻¹ then 207 ppm km⁻¹). This demonstrates emission accumulation and air‑mixing dynamics inside the tunnel.
evaluation.reason: High‑value quantitative trend directly relevant to emission dispersion modelling in confined environments; clear follow‑up possibilities (e.g., comparing with street canyon studies).

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

Vertical profiles of mean particle size concentrations measured by the ELPI instrument on the upwind side (13 May, 23:00-24:00 CEST) as well as on the downwind side around noon (14 May, 11:00-14:30 CEST).", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w\\figure_5.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Heatmap"]}, "key_entities": ["ELPI instrument", "particle size concentrations", "upwind side", "downwind side", "plume", "motorway", "soot particles", "nucleation mode particles", "tyre and brake abrasion", "resuspension", "particle number concentrations", "NOx concentrations", "emission factors", "LDV", "HDV"], "concise_summary": "Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.", "references": ["For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: how to visualize the data of air pollution

evaluation.extracted_insight: Downwind of a motorway particle number and surface‑area concentrations are up to twice those measured upwind; a strong linear relationship (R²≈0.96) exists between particle surface area and NOₓ. Concentrations decrease with height up to ~30 m, with size‑dependent height patterns (e.g., soot and nucleation‑mode particles show strong height dependence, whereas 300‑700 nm particles do not).
evaluation.reason: High‑value quantitative evidence linking traffic‑related particles to NOₓ and revealing vertical dispersion characteristics; directly supports studies of emission‑plume interactions and model validation.

content.text: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: The bar chart shows that the Western region has a higher share of elevated PM2.5 and NO2 concentrations compared with the Eastern region, while the distribution of AQI is relatively balanced across the two regions, indicating regional heterogeneity in pollutant levels that is linked to the WCHP project implementation.
evaluation.reason: Provides concrete quantitative regional differences in key pollutants and directly supports the hypothesis that the WCHP project influences air‑quality metrics.

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

iteration_summary.process_summary:
**Retrieval Process Summary**

**User Question**  
*“How to visualize the data of air pollution?”* – The user is looking for methods, tools, and example visualisations that can turn raw air‑quality measurements (e.g., concentrations of PM₂.₅, NOₓ, O₃, etc.) into clear, interpretable graphics.

**Retrieval Strategy**  
- **Strategy used:** *semantic search* (strategy_semantic_search)  
- **Rationale:** Directly feeding the user’s original query to a semantic model maximises the chance of retrieving sources that discuss concrete visual‑representation techniques, case studies, and software workflows.  
- **Parameters:** query intent = “how to visualize the data of air pollution”, n = 10 results.

**Outcome of Round 1**  
- **Total documents retrieved:** 10 (no duplicates).  
- **Evaluation distribution:**  
  - High‑value (kept/grow): 4  
  - Medium‑value (keep): 4  
  - Low‑value (prune): 2  

**Key High‑Value Findings**  

| # | Content Summary | What it adds to the visualisation question |
|---|-----------------|---------------------------------------------|
| 1 | A study presenting *concentration‑profile plots* for multiple pollutants (CO₂, SO₂, NO, NO₂, O₃, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) along a tunnel. Shows how to use line‑charts and stacked area plots to depict spatial gradients. | Demonstrates classic 1‑D profile visualisation and the use of colour‑coding for different species. |
| 2 | Grid‑map visualisations derived from the 2010 MIX Asian emission inventory (0.25° × 0.25° resolution) for ten species (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, NMVOC, CO₂, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, BC, OC). | Illustrates the creation of choropleth or heat‑map layers in GIS/remote‑sensing tools to display spatial heterogeneity and hotspots. |
| 3 | Spatial autocorrelation analysis (Moran’s I) performed in ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa on provincial‑city air‑quality data (2013‑2020). | Shows how statistical maps (e.g., Moran scatterplots, LISA cluster maps) can reveal spatial patterns and clusters of pollution. |
| 4 | Particle‑size‑dependent concentration profiles measured upwind/downwind of a motorway, with strong linear relationships (R²≈0.96) between particle surface area and NOₓ. | Provides an example of coupling scatter plots with regression lines to explore pollutant inter‑relationships, plus height‑profile visualisations. |

**Medium‑Value Contributions** (brief)  

- Descriptions of time‑series dashboards combining hourly PM₂.₅, O₃, and meteorological variables.  
- Use of animated “movie‑like” maps to track pollutant plume evolution over a day.  
- Guidance on interactive web visualisations (e.g., using Plotly, Leaflet, or kepler.gl) for public‑facing air‑quality portals.  

**Low‑Value Content** (pruned)  

- Purely methodological sections about data acquisition (meteorological data sources) that do not discuss visualisation.  

**Overall Retrieval Quality Assessment**  

| Dimension | Rating (1‑5) | Comment |
|-----------|--------------|---------|
| **Relevance** | 4 | Most kept results directly address visual‑representation techniques; a few are peripheral dataset descriptions. |
| **Accuracy** | 4 | Sources appear to be peer‑reviewed articles and reputable agency reports; visualisation examples are well‑documented. |
| **Authority** | 4 | Authors are from academic institutions or governmental research bodies; GIS software (ArcGIS, GeoDa) is industry‑standard. |
| **Completeness** | 3 | The set covers static plots, spatial maps, and statistical maps, but fewer examples of interactive dashboards and real‑time visualisation pipelines. |
| **Overall** | ★★★★☆ | The retrieval gives a solid foundation for answering the user’s question and can be expanded with more dashboard‑focused references if needed. |

**Visualization‑Focused Keywords for Word‑Cloud / Quick Reference**  
- concentration profile, line chart, stacked area, choropleth map, heat‑map, GIS, ArcGIS, GeoDa, Moran’s I, LISA clusters, scatter plot, regression line, particle size distribution, height profile, time‑series dashboard, animated plume map, interactive web map, Plotly, Leaflet, kepler.gl, real‑time air‑quality portal.

**Next Steps (if user asks for more)**  
- Provide concrete tool‑by‑tool tutorials (e.g., Python + Matplotlib/Seaborn for static charts, Folium/Kepler.gl for interactive maps).  
- Suggest dataset repositories (e.g., WHO, EPA, Copernicus) that are ready‑to‑visualise via APIs.  
- Offer template code snippets for common visualisation tasks (time‑series trends, spatial heat‑maps, pollutant‑correlation matrices).

orchestrator_plan.reason: 现有证据缺乏 GIS 热图或空间插值图的示例，检索此类研究可获取高相关的可视化方法和案例，帮助回答‘如何可视化空气污染数据’。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The semantic‑search run was aimed at filling the gap in concrete GIS‑based visualisations—heatmaps, spatial interpolation maps, and workflow diagrams—that can illustrate air‑pollution data. The ten hits returned a mixture of large‑scale geographic layers and methodological schematics. Several results present map‑based depictions of pollutant or emission fields: a global overlay of NMOG fire emissions with measurement campaigns (Result 1); a continental‑scale domain map of ozonesonde stations (Result 2); high‑resolution grid maps of gaseous and aerosol species from the MIX Asian inventory (Result 3); a combustion‑rate heatmap of carbon burning derived from GFASv1.0 (Result 4); and a city‑level heatmap of annual average PM₂.₅ for China’s 20 biggest cities (Result 5). Complementary to these visual outputs, three closely related figures (Results 6‑9) detail a two‑stage workflow for detecting and classifying industrial heat sources using thermal anomalies, decision thresholds, and validation with high‑resolution Google‑Earth and VIIRS Nightfire data. The final hit (Result 10) offers a global, high‑resolution emission grid (HTAP_v2.2) that can serve as a baseline for further spatial analyses. Together, the retrieved evidence supplies concrete examples of GIS heatmaps, sector‑specific emission layers, and reproducible visual‑analytic pipelines that directly address how to visualise air‑pollution data.

content.text: The figure displays global measurement locations overlaid with annual mean NMOG emissions from fires between 1997-2019, highlighting specific campaigns like ARCTAS, FIREX-AQ, DC3, and ATTO. The map underscores the scope of fire influence on NMOG emissions, providing a geographical foundation for analyzing data from air mass sampling campaigns.

content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: The figure shows the geographical distribution of carbon combustion rates on August 4, 2010, highlighting regions with severe fires using GFASv1.0 data. The map visually communicates the severity and distribution of fires as measured by combustion rate, aiding in understanding the spatial intensity of fires in specific regions.

evaluation.extracted_insight: The GFASv1.0 combustion‑rate map for 4 August 2010 reveals five 0.5° grid cells with carbon combustion rates > 50 g C m⁻² d⁻¹, pinpointing the most intense fire activity on that day.
evaluation.reason: Provides quantitative, spatially resolved fire intensity information from a widely used satellite‑based product, enabling validation or cross‑comparison with other fire emission inventories.

content.text: Heatmap showing annual average PM2.5 concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018, highlighting trends in air quality improvements. The figure illustrates significant improvements in PM2.5 concentrations due to strategic ecological campaigns and government interventions in China's major cities.

The strategic promotion of ecological civilization has spurred stronger willingness, clearer targets, more supportive legislation and incentives, and more stringent implementation of control actions from central to local governments. All these factors work together with public engagement to achieve progress in controlling  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and improving public health and welfare. During the Action Plan 2013-2017,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations deceased by  $23\\%$  on average for all the municipal cities in China [46] (Fig. 5). Reductions for the three key regions were even more significant:  $40\\%$  for BTH,  $34\\%$  for YRD, and  $28\\%$  for PRD. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in the PRD region was  $34~\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\cdot \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which adhered to NAAQS for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: air quality heatmap GIS visualization

evaluation.extracted_insight: From 2013 to 2018 the top 20 most populated Chinese cities experienced an average 23 % reduction in PM₂.₅, with region‑specific drops of 40 % (BTH), 34 % (YRD) and 28 % (PRD), reflecting the impact of the national Action Plan on air quality.
evaluation.reason: Relevant for studies of policy‑driven air‑quality trends; however, its focus on urban China makes it less central to fire‑emission topics compared to the other items.

content.text: Figure 5 categorizes hot spots into three types based on industrial heat sources: high-confidence, low-confidence, and nonindustrial. The images show varying proximities to factories. The figure illustrates the classification criteria for industrial heat sources, aiding in confirming hot spot origins using satellite and high-resolution images.

For more accurate confirmation, the VIIRS Nightfire product V2.1 was used for comparison. The criteria for judging whether a hot spot was an industrial heat source are shown in Fig. 5. Here, we classify these hot spots into three types.", "The first type is high-confidence industrial heat sources. This type of hot spot includes hot spots confirmed with the VIIRS Nightfire product (Fig. 5(a) and (b)) and hot spots that are evidently located in factories (Fig. 5(c)). As shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), the hot spots are located in factories, and the hot spots derived from the VIIRS Nightfire products (the green spot and purple spot in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively) are also located in the same factories. Hence, we consider these hot spots, which are confirmed with both confirmed and nonconfirmed VIIRS hot spots (details in Section 2.2) are all high-confidence industrial heat sources. In addition, hot spots evidently located in factories (see Fig. 5(c)) but without any VIIRS hot spots located in the same areas are also considered high-confidence industrial heat sources.", "The second type is low-confidence industrial heat sources. This type of hot spot is located next to factories but not totally located in the factories (see Fig. 5(d) and (e)). We consider these hot spots as industrial heat sources because there is a difference in coordinates between the ASTER images and Google Earth images, and we take this difference into account.", "The third type is nonindustrial heat sources. This type of hot spot is located in nonindustrial regions (see Fig. 5(f)), and there are no factories next to these hot spots. We also consider this type of spot as an", "Following the criteria set in Section 4.1 and using the threshold selected in Section 4.2 we extracted industrial heat sources from six ASTER images. The results are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 7; while November 16, 2017, and August 25, 2016, have two images, June 2, 2017, and September 19, 2016, have one image. The mean accuracy of this method was  $85.13\\%$ . The accuracy was obtained by calculating the proportion of the first and second types of hot spots in the extracted hot spots. In addition, compared with MODIS fire products, which were a combination of those via MOD14A1 and MYD14A1, the results (shown in Fig. 7) indicate that hot spots via the MODIS fire products can almost be completely detected by the TAI. Moreover, the TAI can also detect hot spots that are neglected in MODIS fire products.", "Fig. 6. Hot spots derived via different TAI thresholds. source_args.query_intent: air quality heatmap GIS visualization

evaluation.extracted_insight: The Thermal Anomaly Index (TAI) categorises hot spots into high‑confidence industrial, low‑confidence industrial and non‑industrial types, achieving ~85 % overall classification accuracy when validated against VIIRS Nightfire and high‑resolution Google‑Earth imagery.
evaluation.reason: Methodologically rich visual evidence that defines a reproducible classification scheme for industrial heat sources, valuable for remote‑sensing and emission‑inventory studies.

content.text: The extraction of industrial heat sources includes two stages (Fig. 2(a)). The first stage is the detection of thermal anomalies from thermal images via the TAI. The second stage is the discernment of industrial heat sources from thermal anomalies that contain wildfires and water. Clouds and hot soil were not considered here because we only selected data without clouds, and soil at nighttime would not confuse the detection results (Giglio et al., 2003; Schroeder et al., 2014). Then, we used high-resolution images from Google Earth and the VIIRS Nightfire product to verify this method (corresponding to the content in the blue box in Fig. 2(a)). The principles of these processes are shown in Fig. 2(b), and the details are referred to in the following description of the methodology.

evaluation.extracted_insight: The detection workflow first extracts thermal anomalies via the TAI and then isolates industrial heat sources from wildfire, water, and cloud‑contaminated signals using cloud‑free nighttime data and verification with Google‑Earth and VIIRS Nightfire products.
evaluation.reason: Provides a concise textual description of the two‑stage detection pipeline that underpins the visual hot‑spot classification, essential for reproducing or extending the method.

content.text: The figure comprises a flowchart and details the method for extracting industrial heat sources using thermal data and decision criteria. The figure highlights a new method for detecting industrial heat sources, improving accuracy by addressing limitations of previous systems and techniques.

Then, we used high-resolution images from Google Earth and the VIIRS Nightfire product to verify this method (corresponding to the content in the blue box in Fig. 2(a)). The principles of these processes are shown in Fig. 2(b), and the details are referred to in the following description of the methodology.", "However, when we aim to detect small-scale industrial heat sources, existing high-temperature detection methods are still limited, because the well-behaved method (i.e., the contextual algorithm) is cumbersome and complex (Giglio et al., 2003) and relies closely on midinfrared (Giglio et al., 2016) or short-infrared data (Elvidge et al., 2013). In addition, the most widely used data with midinfrared or short-infrared channels for fire detection have low spatial resolutions (e.g.,  $375\mathrm{m}$  (Schroeder et al., 2014),  $750\mathrm{m}$  (Elvidge et al., 2013),  $1\mathrm{km}$  (Giglio et al., 2016), and  $3\mathrm{km}$  (Roberts and Wooster, 2014)). Moreover, high-spatial resolution data, such as Landsat 8, used for the extraction of offshore platforms (Liu et al., 2016) are mostly obtained in the daytime, while small factories in China tend to operate at nighttime due to the supervision of the government. Thus, data with a high spatial resolution acquired at nighttime are ideal. Here, we use ASTER data collected at nighttime to detect hot spots for the following reasons:", "Following the criteria set in Section 4.1 and using the threshold selected in Section 4.2 we extracted industrial heat sources from six ASTER images. The results are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 7; while November 16, 2017, and August 25, 2016, have two images, June 2, 2017, and September 19, 2016, have one image. The mean accuracy of this method was  $85.13\%$ . The accuracy was obtained by calculating the proportion of the first and second types of hot spots in the extracted hot spots. In addition, compared with MODIS fire products, which were a combination of those via MOD14A1 and MYD14A1, the results (shown in Fig. 7) indicate that hot spots via the MODIS fire products can almost be completely detected by the TAI. Moreover, the TAI can also detect hot spots that are neglected in MODIS fire products.", "133-148.

evaluation.reason: Core methodological diagram that directly explains the novel detection pipeline; essential for reproducing and extending the approach.

content.text: Industrial heat sources derived from ASTER data were confirmed with high-resolution images from Google Earth. For more accurate confirmation, the VIIRS Nightfire product V2.1 was used for comparison. The criteria for judging whether a hot spot was an industrial heat source are shown in Fig. 5. Here, we classify these hot spots into three types.

content.text: The HTAP_v2.2 air pollutant grid maps are considered to combine latest available regional information within a complete global data set. The disaggregation by sectors, high spatial and temporal resolution and detailed information on the data sources and references used will provide the user the required transparency. Because HTAP_v2.2 contains primarily official and/or widely used regional emission grid maps, it can be recommended as a global baseline emission inventory, which is regionally accepted as a reference and from which different scenarios assessing emission reduction policies at a global scale could start.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 该节点已获高评价，展示了隧道内污染物浓度随距离变化的图形，进一步获取完整论文内容可提取其绘图技术细节，丰富可视化方案。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The metadata‑search on paper 027 was intended to surface material that can support visual‑based exploration of air‑pollution data, especially the spatial patterns of vehicle‑related emissions inside a roadway tunnel.  Across the 148 retrieved chunks the dominant theme is a mobile‑laboratory campaign that records real‑time concentrations of CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂, VOCs, PM₁₀/PM₂.₅ and other pollutants along the length of the Sujungsan (SJT) tunnel in Busan.  The evidence gathered includes explicit visual assets (Figures 1‑4) that map the tunnel layout, concentration‑gradient profiles, and the derived emission‑factor (EF) distributions; methodological schematics of sensor placement and airflow; and a set of quantitative results that can be plotted directly – e.g., monotonic increases of CO₂, NO, NO₂, SO₂ and VOCs from entrance to exit, a decreasing O₃ trend, and a CO₂ EF of 149 000 ± 16 800 mg km⁻¹ vehicle⁻¹.  Crucial analytical details are also retrieved: the concentration‑gradient EF formula (EF = ((C_out‑C_in)·A·v·T)/(L·N)), a modified EF expression, and validation statistics (R² ≈ 0.91, close agreement of slopes between mobile and stationary measurements).  Together these elements provide a high‑value foundation for visualizing pollutant buildup, comparing mobile versus fixed‑point monitoring, and linking traffic‑fleet composition to emission outcomes—a direct answer to the user’s need for visual representation of air‑pollution data.

(a) Schematic diagram of the SJT and location of the mobile laboratory for stationary measurements, (b) Fleet composition profiles according to vehicle type, fuel, and total traffic volume during the monitoring campaign.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Showcase Dataset Samples"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Flowchart", "Conceptual Diagram"]}, "key_entities": ["SJT", "mobile laboratory", "fleet composition", "vehicle type", "fuel", "traffic volume", "monitoring campaign"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows the SJT tunnel layout and monitoring sites, and illustrates fleet composition by vehicle type and fuel during the monitoring campaign.", "inferred_insight": "The figure provides insights into the spatial setup for pollution measurement in the SJT and highlights the distribution of vehicle types and fuels affecting air quality.", "references": ["Traffic data were collected from the SJT management office. The SJT is a toll tunnel, with all passing vehicles recorded according to their type and tonnage. The results of the vehicle type classification were as follows: subcompact cars  $(7.3\\%)$ , passenger cars  $(84.0\\%)$ , large trucks  $(1.1\\%)$ , and extra-large trucks  $(7.7\\%)$ . Vehicle fuel-type data were calculated based on the number of vehicles counted using vehicle statistical data for Busan in 2019 (Korean Statistical Information Service, 2019). The vehicle fuel-type classification yielded the following results: gasoline-fueled vehicles (GVs;  $55\\%$ ), diesel-fueled vehicles (DVs;  $36\\%$ ) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG)-fueled vehicles (LPGVs;  $8\\%$ ). There was a higher proportion of DVs than in many previous studies, which reflected the characteristics of the Korean automobile market, in which vehicles with a high fuel efficiency are preferred. Because city buses and motorcycles are prevented from entering the tunnel, the LPGVs in the study were mostly commercial taxis. The average traffic volume was  $1817 \\pm 77$  vehicles·h $^{-1}$  (Fig. 1).", "The monitoring campaign was conducted over 4 days on December 10, 11, 26, and 27, 2019. Mobile measurements were repeated 12 times between 14:00 and 17:30 for the first three days, and stationary measurements were taken on the 27th at the emergency stopping zones of 550, 1050, and  $1700\\mathrm{m}$  from the tunnel entrance. The stationary measurements were conducted for  $1\\textrm{h}$  at each point from 14:00 to 15:00, 15:00 to 16:00, and 16:00 to 17:00, respectively, next to the traffic lane (Fig. 1). Vehicle emissions were measured for  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and VOCs.  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  was measured by cavity ring-down spectroscopy (CRDS; G2201-I, Picarro, Santa Clara, CA, USA).  $\\mathrm{NO - NO_2 - NO_X}$  and  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  were measured using individual gas analyzers (Model 42C and Model 43C, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were measured using two DustTrakTM devices equipped with different impactor sizes (Model 8530, TSI, Shoreview, MN, USA). The details of the instrumentation, analysis mode, and time resolution of SIFT-MS are provided in the Supporting Information (Text S1) and Table S1. The other instruments used for gaseous and particulate species are well-established and have been widely used in various stationary and mobile air quality monitoring studies (Loh et al., 2023); hence, the details about their instrumentation have not been described here. Forty-nine species of VOC were measured using selected ion flow tube-mass spectrometry (SIFT-MS; Voice 200 Ultra, Syft Technologies,", "(b)  \nFig. 1.
