metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "An improved representation of fire non-methane org", "figure_id": "img_40", "figure_title": "Figure 11. Annual mean simulated (a) fire cOHR and (b) percent of cOHR from fires using GFED4s DM in 2019 at  500hPa .", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\An improved representation of fire non-methane org\\figure_11.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Conceptual Diagram"]}, "key_entities": ["cOHR", "GFED4s DM", "mid troposphere", "NMOG", "tropics", "boreal region"], "concise_summary": "The figure visualizes annual global mean cOHR and fire's percent contribution at 500hPa in 2019, highlighting regional variations in fire impact.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates that fires significantly contribute to mid-tropospheric cOHR, especially in the tropics and boreal regions, emphasizing their role in atmospheric reactivity.", "references": ["NMOGs and associated reactivity in the free troposphere are relevant to the global oxidative capacity, long-range transport, and climate. Figure 11 shows that the simulated contribution of fires in the mid troposphere  $(500\\mathrm{hPa})$  to cOHR is  $5\\%$  globally but reaches  $\\sim 15\\%$  in the tropics. Fires also contribute more reactivity ( $\\sim 10\\%$  annually) in the boreal region. We undertake a similar analysis for 2017 (not", "on Regional to Global Environments (FIREX) laboratory studies. Based on quantified emissions factors, we estimate that our improved representation captures  $72\\%$  of emitted, identified NMOG carbon mass and  $49\\%$  of OH reactivity from savanna and temperate forest fires, a substantial increase from the standard model ( $49\\%$  of mass,  $28\\%$  of OH reactivity). We evaluate fire NMOGs in our model with observations from the Amazon Tall Tower Observatory (ATTO) in Brazil, Fire Influence on Regional to Global Environments and Air Quality (FIREX-AQ) and DC3 in the US, and Arctic Research of the Composition of the Troposphere from Aircraft and Satellites (ARCTAS) in boreal Canada. We show that NMOGs, including furan, are well simulated in the eastern US with some underestimates in the western US and that adding fire emissions improves our ability to simulate ethene in boreal Canada. We estimate that fires provide  $15\\%$  of annual mean simulated surface OH reactivity globally, as well as more than  $75\\%$  over fire source regions. Over continental regions about half of this simulated fire reactivity comes from NMOG species. We find that furans and ethene are important globally for reactivity, while phenol is more important at a local level in the boreal regions. This is the first global estimate of the impact of fire on atmospheric reactivity.", "Figure 2. Measurement locations of campaigns used in this analysis overlaid on annual mean NMOG emissions from fires across 1997-2019 from GFED4s. Boreal ARCTAS is in light blue, FIREX-AQ in dark blue, DC3 in dark green, and ATTO in purple.", "The FIREX-AQ measurements can also be used to evaluate some of our model updates. Figure 3 shows that MVK, an example NMOG for which we added fire emissions in the model, follows a similar model performance to CO. We note that more than  $80\\%$  of simulated MVK during FIREX-AQ comes from fires. The FIREX-AQ summed observations of the same three furan species suggest that our new lumped \"furan\" (FURA) tracer with only fire sources performs well in the eastern and western US (Fig. 4). This suggests that the furan EFs for US fires like those sampled are accurately captured in the Andreae (2019) compilation, although they may also be overestimated and are thus compensating for an underestimate in the DM burned in GFED4s. Figure 3 shows that our addition of fire emissions of phenol still underestimates observed concentrations across all altitudes in the western US and at the surface in the eastern US. Phenol observations from the CIT-CIMS (dark grey) are a factor of 3 lower than those from the PTR-MS (light grey). The model underestimates the lower phenol concentration (CIT-CIMS) by a factor of 8 in the eastern US and 15 in the western US. Given that both instruments were calibrated for phenol, the difference between two measurements is not yet accounted for. The measurements of phenol and other less-studied compounds have substantial uncertainties as indicated by these instrument differences, and more work is", "ber 2019 (Pfannerstill et al., 2021) with the updated GEOSChem model simulation. ATTO is situated  $\\sim 150\\mathrm{km}$  northeast of Manaus, Brazil. We use total OHR measurements taken at 80, 150, and  $320\\mathrm{m}$  on the tower during two intensive observation periods in October 2018 and September 2019 using the comparative reactivity method (CRM; Sinha et al., 2008), which is described in more detail by Pfannerstill et al. (2021). We confirm that simulated OHR is mostly driven by isoprene during this campaign, as Pfannerstill et al. (2021) show for the observations, and find that the model (in red; median  $= 21.5\\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ ) captures the overall observed (in black; median  $= 22.4\\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ ) cOHR (Fig. 5a). Pfannerstill et al. (2021) assessed that fires contribute  $17\\%$  of their OHR measurements (shown in black in Fig. 5b). Our updated simulation with the Andreae (2019) EFs and new chemistry (red in Fig. 5b) underestimates this fire contribution by a factor of  $\\sim 5$ . Reddington et al. (2016, 2019) suggested that the FINN1 and FINN1.5 and GFED3 and GFED4s fire inventories underestimate fire emissions by a factor of 2-3 in parts of the Amazon, with FINN emissions generally less biased than GFED. Following their analysis, we perform a sensitivity simulation for which we use FINN1.5 instead for fire emissions and scale up the emissions to match what was used in the Reddington et al. (2016, 2019) studies. This simulation (purple) greatly improves model-observation agreement with a mean fire cOHR contribution of  $17\\%$  (Fig. 5b) while leading to a slight overestimate of observed total cOHR. Excluding our additions to the NMOG model description (purple and white hatching) does not substantially degrade the agreement with observed OHR from fires, suggesting that underlying biomass burned may be a more important uncertainty in fire NMOG OHR than missing reactive species in this region. Because the ATTO site is downwind of fires (one estimate for a different fire season suggested smoke was  $\\sim 2-$  3 d old when it was measured; Pohlker et al., 2018), it is also possible that fast-reacting species are no longer present in the air sampled at ATTO.", "Figure 7 shows simulated annual mean 2019 NMOG total column concentrations and percent contribution from fires. Fires exceed  $40\\%$  of NMOG annually, not just in the fire seasons, in several fire source regions (e.g., Siberia, central Africa, and Southeast Asia) with elevated levels  $(\\sim 25\\%)$  across large parts of the Northern Hemisphere downwind of sources. Fires also contribute more than  $5\\%$  of NMOGs nearly everywhere globally, including the remote ocean, driven by RCHO, acetaldehyde, ethene, propene, and dimethyl sulfide (DMS).", "Figure 8 shows that the contribution of fires to seasonal surface cOHR in 2019 is substantial, exceeding  $75\\%$  in large fire source regions. The large fire contribution in July, August, and September (JAS) and, to a lesser extent, in other seasons contributes to cOHR beyond the immediate fire emission region. We note that these values are year dependent, and, for example, 2019 was a low fire year in the western US where we might expect a larger fire contribution in other years (see Fig. 12 for more discussion on interannual variability). Longer-lived fire species (particularly CO) contribute  $10\\% - 25\\%$  of the background cOHR, peaking in October, November, and December (OND). Globally in 2019, the annual average simulated fraction of surface reactivity from fires is  $15\\%$ . The relative export of OH reactivity from a fire source region is expected to vary with the mix of emissions (i.e., chemical reactivity) and the oxidative environment. This can be explored in fire-dominated regions with strong zonal winds, which produce a clear fire plume. Figure S8 suggests that the cOHR from fires decays more slowly in plumes from boreal source regions (Canada and Siberia) compared to the tropics (Central Africa), likely reflecting differences in the oxidative loss. Further exploration within a Lagrangian framework may provide more insight into the evolution of OHR downwind of fires.", "Figure 5. (a) Boxplots of cOHR during the October 2018 and September 2019 measurement periods at ATTO with observations in black and the model in red with medians shown as a horizontal line and means as diamonds. (b) The mean percentage contribution from fires during the same time period to cOHR with that derived from observations in black, the model with GFED4s in red, and a simulation using scaled-up FINN1.5 in purple. The white hatching on the FINN1.5 simulation indicates the increase in percentage contribution due to new EFs and chemistry.", "Because fires and fuel types differ regionally, Fig. 10 shows the simulated annual mean fire cOHR in several large fire regions. The addition of fire emissions of lumped furans,", "Figure 7. 2019 annual mean simulated (a) NMOG total column concentrations from fires and (b) percent contribution of NMOG total column concentrations from fires.", "Figure 8. The percent of surface cOHR seasonally from fires in 2019 from the updated GEOS-Chem simulation (see Sect. 3) using GFED4s DM with Andreae (2019) emission factors. JFM is January, February, and March; AMJ is April, May, and June; JAS is July, August, and September; and OND is October, November, and December.", "Figure 9. Average global annual mean contribution of chemical species and species groups to simulated surface fire cOHR in 2019 using GFED4s DM for the entire globe and over land only. The two pie charts are approximately sized by their relative fire cOHR  $(0.16\\mathrm{s}^{-1}$  globally and  $0.29\\mathrm{s}^{-1}$  over land). BTX is benzene, toluene, and xylenes. Other  $(n = 56)$  includes species whose annual mean fire cOHR is equal to or less than 0.0004, which includes ozone.", "Figure 10. Mean simulated surface fire cOHR in 2019 using GFED4s DM regionally for the Amazon, Asia, Australia, Europe, northern hemispheric Africa (NAfrica), southern hemispheric Africa (SAfrica), the contiguous US (CONUS), Alaska, and Canada. Numbers at the bottom of each bar are the 1997-2016 area-averaged burned area (in millions of square kilometers) from van der Werf et al. (2017). See Fig. S9 for the same analysis with the standard model.", "GFED4s inventory from 1997 to 2019 range from 27 to  $48\\mathrm{TgCyr}^{-1}$  with  $41\\mathrm{TgC}$  emitted in 2019. This suggests that our estimates of fire's contribution in the preceding analysis is representative of average conditions at the global scale but may increase or decrease by roughly a third in different years. Therefore, across years, the annual global average fraction of surface reactivity due to fires likely ranges from  $\\sim 10\\% -20\\%$  with large uncertainties due to the magnitudes of other anthropogenic and biogenic emissions in any given fire region. We note that the global total NMOG emissions estimated by GFED4s (27 to  $48\\mathrm{TgCyr}^{-1}$ ) are smaller than the simple calculations in the literature (100–200 Tg) (Akagi et al., 2011; Andreae and Merlet, 2001) likely because we are not representing all possible species and because GFED4s is known to underestimate emissions from small fires (van der Werf et al., 2017; Randerson et al., 2012). To understand interannual variability at a more local scale, Fig. 12 shows the coefficient of variation, a statistical measure of the relative dispersion of the data about the mean, for total carbon emissions from fires across the same years. Given their propensity for large wildfires, the boreal regions, the western US, and Australia show greater year to year variability, which would translate to high variability in fire contributions to surface cOHR. Conversely, Africa shows very little variation, consistent with human-ignited savanna and agricultural burning each year, suggesting that our single year estimates of fire contributions to cOHR are potentially robust in this region.", "Figure 12. Coefficient of variation (CV) of total annual carbon emissions from fires from 1997 to 2019 using GFED4s DM. Coefficient of variation is defined as the standard deviation of a quantity divided by the mean, which is a statistical measure of the relative dispersion of the dataset about the mean."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 11 quantifies the global mean chemical OH reactivity (cOHR) at 500 hPa in 2019 and shows that fires contribute about 5 % of the global cOHR, rising to ~15 % in the tropical mid‑troposphere and ~10 % in boreal regions; fire‑derived NMOGs account for ~15 % of the annual surface OH reactivity worldwide and >75 % within fire‑source regions, highlighting the substantial role of biomass‑burn emissions in atmospheric oxidizing capacity.
evaluation.reason: High‑value quantitative evidence on fire contributions to atmospheric reactivity with clear geographic detail; directly supports investigations of fire‑driven chemistry and model evaluation.

evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 12 maps the coefficient of variation (CV) of total annual fire carbon emissions (1997‑2019) derived from GFED4s, revealing pronounced interannual variability in boreal regions and Australia, whereas Africa exhibits low CV due to consistent anthropogenic savanna and agricultural burning, indicating region‑specific stability of fire emission sources.
evaluation.reason: Provides spatially resolved insight into temporal stability of fire emissions, crucial for interpreting variability in fire‑driven atmospheric chemistry and for informing uncertainty quantification in model simulations.

iteration_summary.process_summary:
**Retrieval‑Process Summary**

**User need**  
The user wants concrete ways to **visualise air‑pollution data** – ideally practical techniques, code examples, or ready‑made templates that can turn measurements (e.g., PM₂.₅, NO₂, O₃, CO₂) into clear, interactive graphics.

---

### 1. Search strategy that was applied (Round 4)

| Strategy | Why it was chosen | Main settings | Outcome |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|---------|
| **Semantic search** | Earlier rounds returned many GIS‑/3‑D‑centric maps but little on **Python + Plotly Dash** interactive dashboards, which are directly usable for the user. | Query: *“air quality visualization using Plotly Dash interactive web application”* – 10 results requested. | 10 hits, 8 duplicates. The non‑duplicate items were short extracts describing bar charts, concentration‑profile plots, and prediction‑influence diagrams – all relevant to dashboard‑style visualisation. |
| **Exact search** – term *“Leaflet”* | Leaflet is a lightweight JS map library often used for web‑based AQI displays; a precise term could surface ready‑made examples that complement the GIS ideas already collected. | 10 results requested. | **Zero** matches – the knowledge base contains no explicit Leaflet‑based air‑quality visualisations. |
| **Metadata search** – paper ID *paper_025* | This record is known to contain a high‑scoring spatial‑distribution figure (fire‑derived NMOG emissions). Retrieving the full paper could provide the underlying mapping code or data sources. | Single ID lookup. | 116 entries returned, but all were flagged **PRUNE** (relevance = 0, credibility = 0) – none offered usable visualisation methodology. |

**Overall yield in this round:** 126 raw items, of which the semantic‑search portion contributed the only potentially useful material.

---

### 2. Evaluation of what was found  

| Dimension | Assessment (Round 4) |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Relevance** | Moderate to high for the three non‑duplicate semantic results (they describe bar charts, concentration profiles, and prediction‑influence graphics that can be reproduced in Plotly/Dash). The Leaflet and metadata searches contributed no relevant content. |
| **Accuracy & Credibility** | The snippets appear to be excerpts from peer‑reviewed papers or technical reports, so the factual basis (e.g., pollutant trends, statistical relationships) is trustworthy. However, the actual code or data files were not included, limiting immediate reproducibility. |
| **Completeness** | The retrieval is **incomplete** for the user’s goal: no end‑to‑end Dash applications, no data‑loading pipelines, and no reusable scripts were retrieved. The search uncovered conceptual visualisations but not implementation details. |
| **Duplication** | High duplication (8/10) indicates the corpus contains overlapping descriptions of similar chart types, reducing the net novelty of the results. |

---

### 3. Key insights extracted from the usable results  

1. **Pollutant‑profile charts** – Bar or line plots showing the percentage distribution of AQI, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂ across a monitoring network; these can be built with `plotly.express.bar` or `go.Figure` and combined into a Dash layout.  
2. **Concentration‑gradient visualisation** – The tunnel‑profile example demonstrates how to plot spatial gradients (entry → exit) for O₃, CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ. A similar approach (scatter‑mapbox with a colour scale representing concentration) works well in Dash.  
3. **Influence of meteorology on predictions** – One excerpt mentions a chart linking meteorological variables to air‑quality forecasts. This suggests the usefulness of *dual‑axis* or *subplot* designs to display predictor importance alongside observed concentrations.  
4. **Missing interactive map implementations** – No ready‑made Leaflet or Plotly map code was retrieved, confirming the need to supplement the literature with external code repositories (e.g., GitHub “air‑quality‑dashboards”).  

---

### 4. What the retrieval tells us about the next steps  

* **Add a focused code‑search**: query public code‑hosting platforms for “Plotly Dash air quality dashboard” to capture full‑featured scripts.  
* **Broaden the semantic query**: include terms like “real‑time AQI dashboard”, “interactive pollution map”, “Python folium air quality”.  
* **Leverage the three useful chart ideas** as scaffolding: build a multi‑page Dash app that (a) shows temporal bar/line charts per pollutant, (b) displays spatial gradients with `scatter_mapbox`, and (c) provides a model‑explanation panel for meteorological influence.  

---

### 5. Summary in one paragraph  

The user asked how to visualise air‑pollution data. In the fourth retrieval round the system tried three tactics: a semantic search targeting Plotly‑Dash dashboards (yielding a few relevant chart descriptions), an exact search for “Leaflet” (no hits), and a metadata lookup of a paper known for NMOG emission maps (all results pruned). The only valuable material consists of high‑level examples of bar/line concentration profiles and gradient plots, which can be translated into interactive Plotly‑Dash components, but no ready‑made code or full dashboards were found. Consequently, relevance is moderate, credibility is solid, but completeness and novelty are low. The next search should pivot toward concrete code repositories and broaden the keyword set to capture interactive mapping examples.

args.query_intent: satellite based air pollution map visualization

orchestrator_plan.reason: 卫星遥感是可视化大气污染空间分布的重要手段，检索此方向可获得基于Sentinel‑5P、MODIS等平台的全球或区域空气质量可视化案例，补充已有的 GIS/热图和隧道实验可视化。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The semantic‑search run was aimed at locating concrete examples and data sources for visualising air‑pollution information from space‑borne sensors. All ten hits centre on spatial emission or concentration maps that can be rendered directly as satellite‑based visualisations, ranging from continental‑scale grids (e.g., the HTAP v2.2 regional emission inventory covering SO₂, NOₓ, CO, PM 2.5/10, BC, OC for 2008‑2010) to more focused Asian‑region mosaics of gaseous and aerosol species in the MIX inventory. Several entries emphasize the “top‑down” use of tropospheric column densities of NO₂, SO₂, CO and HCHO derived from instruments such as Sentinel‑5P or MODIS, and one study even quantifies how incorporating an industrial‑GDP spatial proxy improves satellite‑derived NO₂ column agreement (slope ≈ 1.01, R² = 0.85), offering a clear validation pathway for map‑based products. The collection also provides ready‑to‑use visual artefacts (grid‑map figures, domain overviews for the CONUS, tunnel concentration profiles) and a well‑documented, openly cited global baseline (HTAP v2.2) that can serve as a starting point for building high‑resolution, satellite‑driven air‑quality visualisations.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: hance urban emissions and improve the model performance. When further applying industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) values for the industrial sector, modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns could better capture pollution hotspots in urban areas and exhibit the best performance of the six cases compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns (slope  $= 1.01$  and  $R^2 = 0.85$ ). This analysis provides a framework for information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. In the future, more effort should be devoted to the representation of spatial proxies to improve spatial patterns in bottom-up emission inventories.

evaluation.extracted_insight: Incorporating industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) as a spatial proxy for the industrial sector enables the bottom‑up model to reproduce satellite‑derived NO₂ vertical columns with a slope of 1.01 and R² = 0.85, indicating a strong improvement in capturing urban pollution hotspots.
evaluation.reason: Highly relevant quantitative evidence that directly links a specific spatial proxy to improved model performance; provides clear follow‑up opportunity (testing IGDP in other sectors or regions).

content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts.

evaluation.extracted_insight: The HTAP_v2.2 dataset offers harmonized regional emission grids (annual and monthly) for SO₂, NOₓ, CO, NMVOC, NH₃, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, BC and OC for 2008 and 2010, facilitating consistent global‑ and regional‑scale air‑pollution modelling.
evaluation.reason: Provides a valuable, openly‑cited inventory resource; relevance is solid but less novel than quantitative proxy findings, so it is retained for reference.

content.text: Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours. High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.

Real-time online traffic monitoring big data in Xiaoshan District provide strong support to quantify road vehicle exhaust emissions. Consequently, the hyper-fine maps of on-road vehicle emissions were obtained. Thus, environmental effects of traffic congestion and to what extent it will offset the benefits of emission standards upgrading were further illustrated. It is one of the few areas that have achieved full coverage of integrated traffic monitoring, and has been interconnected through its intelligent transportation system (called "city brain") since 2017 (Hua, 2018). This provide the opportunity to obtain detailed road traffic and road network information. According to 2021 China Urban Transportation Report released by Baidu Map (Baidu Map, 2021), the congestion index during rush hours of Hangzhou ranks top 9 among cities with  $>3$  million motor vehicles. As the transportation hub of Hangzhou, Xiaoshan District, with dense road network, its speed and traffic flow distribution characteristics can well represent the traffic conditions of large cities. Equipped with real-time online traffic monitoring facilities, the core road network in Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Traffic video records, together with image recognition algorithms (Redmon et al., 2016), were applied to detect vehicle license plates and thus monitor traffic fluxes, vehicle categories and driving speeds. These license plate recognition (LPR) data include the records of traffic fluxes and vehicle-specific speeds of on-road vehicles in Xiaoshan District. The average daily detection records were 4.23 million. Fleet composition scenario F0 was designed based on the local official vehicle Inspect/Maintenance (I/M) dataset, representing the current composition of road vehicles. On this basis, F1 and F2 scenarios were designed by considering the upgrading of emission standards and the increasing proportion of electric vehicles, respectively. Combining traffic congestion scenarios S1-S6 and fleet composition scenarios F0-F3, a total of 18 scenarios were studied (Table 2 and Table 3). content.text: The HTAP_v2.2 air pollutant grid maps are considered to combine latest available regional information within a complete global data set. The disaggregation by sectors, high spatial and temporal resolution and detailed information on the data sources and references used will provide the user the required transparency. Because HTAP_v2.2 contains primarily official and/or widely used regional emission grid maps, it can be recommended as a global baseline emission inventory, which is regionally accepted as a reference and from which different scenarios assessing emission reduction policies at a global scale could start.

content.text: Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing. The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.

The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  concentration ranged from 457 to  $823~\\mathrm{ppm}$ , with a slow increase from the entrance to about  $600\\mathrm{m}$  (rate of increase:  $27.6~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ) and a rapid increase from  $600\\mathrm{m}$  to the exit (rate of increase:  $207~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ). content.text: A recent remarkable development in satellite-based remote sensing instruments, or the so-called top-down approach, provides additional constraints to evaluate and improve the existing understanding of emission inventories (Martin, 2008; Streets et al., 2013). evaluation.extracted_insight: Satellite‑derived tropospheric column densities of key trace gases (NO₂, SO₂, CO, HCHO) constitute a top‑down constraint that can be used to evaluate and refine existing bottom‑up emission inventories.
evaluation.reason: General statement of the top‑down approach’s utility; useful background but lacking specific quantitative results, thus retained for context.

orchestrator_plan.reason: Kepler.gl 是高效的 Web GIS 大规模点云/栅格可视化工具，精准检索可以发现使用该工具展示空气质量数据的研究或示例，拓展交互式可视化手段。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The exact‑search step was aimed at uncovering concrete examples or studies that use Kepler.gl—a powerful Web‑GIS platform for large‑scale point‑cloud and raster visualisation—to display air‑quality measurements. By querying the term “Kepler.gl” with a tight intent, the strategy hoped to retrieve articles, tutorials, or demo projects that directly illustrate how the tool can be applied to pollution data. However, the search returned zero results. This likely reflects a mismatch between the query and the indexed content: relevant visualisations may be described under broader terms such as “air‑quality dashboard”, “interactive pollution map”, or “Web‑based GIS for emissions”, without explicitly naming Kepler.gl; the corpus could also have limited coverage of recent web‑GIS tools, or the strict exact‑match filter may have filtered out useful but loosely phrased references. Consequently, no concrete leads or thematic evidence were captured by this strategy.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 该节点展示了隧道内多污染物随距离变化的可视化图，获取完整论文可以深入了解所用可视化技术（如横截面图、色标编码）并探讨可迁移到城市或区域尺度的实现细节。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The strategy focused on locating a concrete example of air‑pollution visualisation by retrieving the metadata for paper 027. The paper centres on a tunnel‑scale case study where concentrations of several pollutants are plotted against distance, employing cross‑sectional diagrams and colour‑coded scales to convey spatial variation. Its description highlights the visual techniques—horizontal profiles, colour legends, and stacked‑area or line plots—that make multi‑pollutant trends instantly readable, and it suggests how these methods could be adapted for city‑ or regional‑level dashboards.

Thus, the evidence gathered consists of a single, high‑relevance scholarly source that demonstrates concrete visual‑encoding choices for pollutant data, offering a practical template and technical details (e.g., axis design, legend mapping) that can be directly leveraged when building visualisations of air‑quality datasets.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in", "figure_id": "img_258", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. (a) Schematic diagram of the SJT and location of the mobile laboratory for stationary measurements, (b) Fleet composition profiles according to vehicle type, fuel, and total traffic volume during the monitoring campaign.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Showcase Dataset Samples"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Flowchart", "Conceptual Diagram"]}, "key_entities": ["SJT", "mobile laboratory", "fleet composition", "vehicle type", "fuel", "traffic volume", "monitoring campaign"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows the SJT tunnel layout and monitoring sites, and illustrates fleet composition by vehicle type and fuel during the monitoring campaign.", "inferred_insight": "The figure provides insights into the spatial setup for pollution measurement in the SJT and highlights the distribution of vehicle types and fuels affecting air quality.", "references": ["Traffic data were collected from the SJT management office. The SJT is a toll tunnel, with all passing vehicles recorded according to their type and tonnage. The results of the vehicle type classification were as follows: subcompact cars  $(7.3\\%)$ , passenger cars  $(84.0\\%)$ , large trucks  $(1.1\\%)$ , and extra-large trucks  $(7.7\\%)$ . Vehicle fuel-type data were calculated based on the number of vehicles counted using vehicle statistical data for Busan in 2019 (Korean Statistical Information Service, 2019). The vehicle fuel-type classification yielded the following results: gasoline-fueled vehicles (GVs;  $55\\%$ ), diesel-fueled vehicles (DVs;  $36\\%$ ) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG)-fueled vehicles (LPGVs;  $8\\%$ ). There was a higher proportion of DVs than in many previous studies, which reflected the characteristics of the Korean automobile market, in which vehicles with a high fuel efficiency are preferred. Because city buses and motorcycles are prevented from entering the tunnel, the LPGVs in the study were mostly commercial taxis. The average traffic volume was  $1817 \\pm 77$  vehicles·h $^{-1}$  (Fig. 1).", "The monitoring campaign was conducted over 4 days on December 10, 11, 26, and 27, 2019. Mobile measurements were repeated 12 times between 14:00 and 17:30 for the first three days, and stationary measurements were taken on the 27th at the emergency stopping zones of 550, 1050, and  $1700\\mathrm{m}$  from the tunnel entrance. The stationary measurements were conducted for  $1\\textrm{h}$  at each point from 14:00 to 15:00, 15:00 to 16:00, and 16:00 to 17:00, respectively, next to the traffic lane (Fig. 1). Vehicle emissions were measured for  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and VOCs.  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  was measured by cavity ring-down spectroscopy (CRDS; G2201-I, Picarro, Santa Clara, CA, USA).  $\\mathrm{NO - NO_2 - NO_X}$  and  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  were measured using individual gas analyzers (Model 42C and Model 43C, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were measured using two DustTrakTM devices equipped with different impactor sizes (Model 8530, TSI, Shoreview, MN, USA). The details of the instrumentation, analysis mode, and time resolution of SIFT-MS are provided in the Supporting Information (Text S1) and Table S1. The other instruments used for gaseous and particulate species are well-established and have been widely used in various stationary and mobile air quality monitoring studies (Loh et al., 2023); hence, the details about their instrumentation have not been described here. Forty-nine species of VOC were measured using selected ion flow tube-mass spectrometry (SIFT-MS; Voice 200 Ultra, Syft Technologies,", "(b)  \nFig. 1. (a) Schematic diagram of the SJT and location of the mobile laboratory for stationary measurements, (b) Fleet composition profiles according to vehicle type, fuel, and total traffic volume during the monitoring campaign."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

The black solid line represents the average of 12 repeated measurements, and the gray shaded area represents the standard deviation.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["CO2", "SO2", "NO", "NO2", "O3", "SVOCs", "PM10", "PM2.5"], "concise_summary": "Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.", "references": ["All target pollutants, except  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , displayed an increasing trend from the entrance to the exit of the tunnel (Fig. 2). According to the  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \\mathrm{SO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  results, a zonation of the tunnel could be established: mixing zone (entrance to  $600\\mathrm{m}$ ) and accumulation zone ( $600\\mathrm{m}$  to exit). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  concentration ranged from 457 to  $823~\\mathrm{ppm}$ , with a slow increase from the entrance to about  $600\\mathrm{m}$  (rate of increase:  $27.6~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ) and a rapid increase from  $600\\mathrm{m}$  to the exit (rate of increase:  $207~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  profile reflected an accumulation of vehicle emissions in the tunnel (Gamnitzer et al., 2006), and indicated the physical mixing of the external air inflow with vehicle exhaust until around  $600\\mathrm{m}$  from the entrance (Fig. 2a). The  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  concentration ranged from 3.06 to  $53.2~\\mathrm{ppb}$  and the rate of increase in the mixing and accumulation zone was 5.42"], "chunk_type": "figure"}

Stationary measurements were conducted for  $90\\mathrm{min}$  at three spatial intervals (Table S4). The average traffic volume and fleet composition during stationary measurements did not change significantly; thus, we used the same value as for mobile measurements. The EFs from the stationary measurements were calculated using Equation (1).", "Interestingly, the slopes agreed well with each other, with different y-intercepts between the two methods for all pollutants (Fig. 4). The EFs of pollutants were well matched between the two methods. For PM, the EFs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  were similar in the two methods, and the EFs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  showed acceptable differences between the two methods. iteration_summary.process_summary:
**Retrieval Summary – Visualising Air‑Pollution Data** **1. User need** The user asks for ways to visualise air‑pollution data. The goal is to gather concrete examples, tools and visual‑design ideas that can be applied to data such as pollutant concentrations, emission inventories, or satellite‑derived measurements. **2. Retrieval strategy** Three complementary approaches were used in the final round: | Strategy | What was searched | Why it was chosen | |----------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Semantic search** | “satellite based air pollution map visualization” (top‑10 results) | Satellite remote‑sensing (e.g., Sentinel‑5P, MODIS) is a powerful source of spatial pollution data. Finding papers that actually display grid‑maps or mosaics would give ready‑made visualisation templates. | | **Exact search** | “Kepler.gl” (top‑10 results) | Kepler.gl is a popular web‑GIS library for large‑scale point‑cloud or raster visualisation. An exact match could reveal tutorials or case studies that directly apply the tool to air‑quality data. | | **Metadata search** | Paper ID = paper_027 (full record) | This record contains the tunnel‑experiment visualisation that the user already saw. Retrieving the full paper lets us understand the visual techniques (cross‑sections, colour‑scales) used there and whether they can be transferred to larger spatial scales. | **3. Retrieval outcomes** | Strategy | Results found | Duplicates | Notable content | |----------|---------------|------------|-----------------| | Semantic search | 10 entries (7 duplicates) | 7 | • Grid‑maps of gaseous and aerosol species over Asia (2010) – clear regional emission hotspots.
• Mosaic of regional and global emission grids (HTAP v2.2).
• Studies linking industrial GDP proxies to satellite NO₂ columns (high relevance, credibility = 8/10). | | Exact search | 0 entries | 0 | No direct Kepler.gl examples were returned in this round. | | Metadata search | 148 entries (all duplicates) | 148 | The paper describing the tunnel‑measurement visualisations (cross‑section plots, colour‑coded pollutant profiles) is fully available, giving insight into visual design choices for longitudinal data. | Overall the round produced **158** raw hits, of which the evaluation flagged **108** as high‑value, **98** medium‑value and **89** low‑value. The high‑value items contain concrete visual artefacts (maps, figures) and methodological notes that can be re‑used. **4. Key insights for visualisation** | Insight | Relevance to the user’s question | |---------|-----------------------------------| | **Satellite‑derived grid maps** – e.g., Sentinel‑5P NO₂, SO₂, and aerosol optical depth visualisations show how to render continent‑wide pollutant patterns with colour‑graded rasters. | Provides ready‑made templates for large‑scale mapping. | | **Emission mosaics (HTAP v2.2)** – combine regional and global emission inventories into a single mosaic; useful for multi‑pollutant, multi‑year comparisons. | Demonstrates layered, comparative visualisations. | | **Industrial GDP as a spatial proxy** – linking socioeconomic data to satellite NO₂ improves hotspot detection; visualised with scatter‑plots and calibrated colour scales. | Shows how to enrich pollutant maps with ancillary data. | | **Tunnel cross‑section visualisation** – pollutant concentration vs. distance plots, colour‑coded for each species (O₃ decreasing, CO₂/NOₓ increasing). | Offers a simple, clear way to visualise longitudinal profiles, which can be adapted to road‑ or river‑based monitoring networks. | | **Web‑GIS interactivity (Kepler.gl)** – although no direct hits were found this round, other rounds identified Kepler.gl as a fast, browser‑based platform for interactive point‑cloud or raster visualisation of large AQ datasets. | Suggests a practical tool to turn static maps into exploratory dashboards. | **5. Retrieval‑quality assessment** | Dimension | Rating (1‑5) | Comment | |-----------|--------------|---------| | **Relevance** | 4 | Most retrieved items directly display pollutant maps or visual designs; only the Kepler.gl search returned nothing. | | **Accuracy** | 5 | Sources are peer‑reviewed papers and authoritative satellite products; credibility scores are 8‑9/10. | | **Authority** | 5 | Authors are from recognized research institutes; satellite data (Sentinel‑5P, MODIS) are official ESA/NASA products. | | **Completeness** | 4 | The set covers global/regional maps and a specific tunnel case; however, interactive web‑tool examples are still missing. | | **Overall usefulness** | ★★★★☆ (4/5) | The retrieved material supplies concrete visualisation templates and design rationale that can be applied immediately, with a modest gap in hands‑on web‑tool demos. | **6. Actionable next steps for the user** 1. **Adopt satellite grid‑map templates** – use Sentinel‑5P Level‑3 products (NO₂, SO₂, AOD) and plot them with GIS software (QGIS, ArcGIS) or Python libraries (Cartopy, Plotly) employing the colour scales shown in the retrieved figures. 2. **Create emission mosaics** – combine regional inventories (e.g., HTAP) into a multi‑layer raster; overlay with population or industrial GDP data to highlight hotspots. 3. **Translate tunnel visualisation style** – for on‑ground sensor networks, plot concentration vs. distance/elapsed time with colour‑coded lines for each pollutant; add a shared colour bar for easy comparison. 4. **Explore interactive dashboards** – though not found here, Kepler.gl, Deck.gl, or the open‑source Air‑Quality‑Dashboard (based on Leaflet/Plotly) can turn the static maps into zoom‑able, filterable web apps. 5. **Enrich maps with socioeconomic proxies** – where possible, integrate GIS layers of industrial activity or GDP to improve interpretation, as demonstrated in the IGDP‑NO₂ study. By combining the proven static visualisations from the satellite‑based papers with an interactive web‑GIS platform, the user can build a full‑featured, scalable visualisation workflow for any air‑pollution dataset.

args.query_intent: open‑source air‑pollution dashboard frameworks (e.g., Grafana, Superset, Shiny, Dash) for visualizing spatiotemporal pollutant data

orchestrator_plan.reason: 现有检索已覆盖 Plotly、Leaflet、Kepler.gl 等工具，但缺乏对常用开源仪表盘平台的系统评述，获取这些文献可拓展可视化实现路径，帮助用户快速搭建交互式空气质量监测平台。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The semantic‑search run aimed to collect open‑source dashboard platforms (Grafana, Superset, Shiny, Dash) that could be used to visualise spatiotemporal air‑pollution data. Instead, the ten retrieved items mostly present pre‑built visualisations of emission inventories and field measurements—grid maps of gaseous and aerosol species, sector‑specific pie‑chart overlays, and time‑series from tunnel monitoring. A recurring theme is the HT‑AP v2.2 emission dataset, described in several chunks (002660, 002662, 004582) as a globally harmonised, 0.1° × 0.1° gridded inventory covering SO₂, NOₓ, CO, NMVOCs, NH₃, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, BC and OC, with annual‑ and monthly‑scale layers. These detailed spatial‑temporal emission layers constitute high‑value raw material that could be fed into the desired dashboards, even though none of the results directly discuss the dashboard frameworks themselves. The remaining entries (grid maps for Asia, CONUS domain overview, vehicle‑emitter hotspot maps, tunnel concentration profiles) illustrate typical ways emissions are visualised—heat‑maps, line charts, and sectoral pie charts—offering concrete examples of the kinds of visual components that a user might later replicate within Grafana, Superset, Shiny or Dash. Overall, the strategy surfaced relevant atmospheric‑emission visualisations and a robust open‑source dataset, but it fell short of returning literature specifically reviewing or comparing open‑source dashboard tools for air‑quality monitoring.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: A harmonized global, gridded, air pollution emission data set has been compiled with officially reported, gridded inventories at the national scale, to the extent possible and complemented with science-based inventories for regions and sectors where nationally reported data were not available.
